name: Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb
name_original: Reinosa, Villacantid, Brañavieja, Espinilla – ida y vuelta
description: Tackle the difficult 37.5-mile Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb, a challenging road cycling route with 3789 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Road cycling around Reinosa offers diverse terrain, characterized by the high Cantabrian Mountains, the Ebro River, and the expansive Ebro Reservoir. This region provides challenging climbs, scenic routes along waterways, and varied landscapes for cyclists. The area features significant elevation changes, with routes traversing gorges and offering panoramic views of the surrounding natural features. Best road cycling routes around Reinosa The most popular road cycling route is Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb, a 37.5 miles (60.3 km) trail that takes 3 hours 20 minutes to complete. This route features a significant climb into the high Cantabrian Mountains. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Source of the Ebro River – Alto del Bardal loop from Río Ebro, a difficult 52.0 miles (83.8 km) path. This route explores the Ebro River's source and includes the notable Alto del Bardal climb, offering extensive mountain scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Aurora Fountain – La Llanuca Roman Ruins loop from Reinosa, a 49.4 miles (79.5 km) trail leading through scenic countryside and past historical sites, often completed in about 3 hours 30 minutes. Road cycling around Reinosa is defined by the Cantabrian Mountains, the Ebro River, and the Ebro Reservoir. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate loops to difficult mountain ascents. The routes in Reinosa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.86 stars from over 18 reviews. More than 620 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Reinosa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of road cycling routes can I find around Reinosa?
A: Reinosa offers a diverse range of road cycling routes, from challenging climbs in the high Cantabrian Mountains to scenic loops around the Ebro Reservoir and routes exploring the Ebro River's source. You'll find options traversing gorges, passing historical sites, and offering panoramic views. There are 32 routes in total, with 4 easy, 15 moderate, and 13 difficult options.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly road cycling routes in Reinosa?
A: Yes, Reinosa has several easier road cycling routes suitable for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed ride. For instance, routes exploring the Historic Center of Reinosa or leading to Fontibre and Villacantid offer mostly paved surfaces and cultural elements. While specific easy routes are not detailed, komoot lists 4 easy road cycling tours in the area.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling climbs or routes near Reinosa?
A: For a significant challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1005123044/alto-campoo-fuente-del-chivo-climb" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb&lt;/a&gt; is a popular difficult route, taking you into the high Cantabrian Mountains. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20160262" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Ebro River – Alto del Bardal loop from Río Ebro&lt;/a&gt;, which includes the notable Alto del Bardal climb and extensive mountain scenery. The region features 13 difficult routes in total.

Q: What natural landmarks or scenic viewpoints can I expect to see on road cycling routes in Reinosa?
A: Road cycling routes around Reinosa offer stunning natural features. You can visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2573626" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Ebro River&lt;/a&gt;, cycle around the expansive Ebro Reservoir, or explore the gorges of the Hoces del Alto Ebro y Rudrón Park. Routes often provide views of the Cantabrian Mountains, and some climbs, like the Tres Mares Peak, offer spectacular panoramas of the Cantabrian interior and even the Picos de Europa.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural attractions accessible by road bike in Reinosa?
A: Yes, several routes combine cycling with cultural exploration. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31470831" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Reinosa – Ruins of Julióbriga loop from Reinosa&lt;/a&gt; takes you past the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3672317" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ruins of Julióbriga&lt;/a&gt;. You can also explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5415244" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Reinosa&lt;/a&gt; itself, with its Baroque church of San Sebastián and buildings in the Town Hall Square.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Reinosa?
A: The road cycling routes in Reinosa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.86 stars from over 18 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, the challenging mountain ascents, and the scenic beauty of the Ebro River and Cantabrian Mountains.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Reinosa?
A: Yes, many routes in Reinosa are designed as loops. For example, the Ebro Reservoir offers a popular circular route. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31480623" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aurora Fountain – La Llanuca Roman Ruins loop from Reinosa&lt;/a&gt; is another excellent circular option, as is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39553338" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aurora Fountain – Alto de Brenes loop from Apeadero Río Ebro&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is it possible to combine road cycling with public transport in Reinosa?
A: Reinosa is connected by public transport, and options like Renfe trains or ALSA buses might allow for bicycle transport, though policies can vary. It's advisable to check with the specific transport provider for their current regulations regarding bikes, especially for longer journeys or during peak times.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Reinosa?
A: Parking is generally available in and around Reinosa. For routes starting near specific attractions, such as the Ebro Reservoir or Fontibre, you can often find designated parking areas. It's recommended to check local signage or specific route descriptions for the most convenient parking spots.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Reinosa?
A: The best time for road cycling in Reinosa is typically during the spring, summer, and early autumn months when the weather is milder and more stable. The region's high mountain passes can be subject to snow and colder temperatures in winter, making some routes less accessible or more challenging.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that pass through forests or natural parks?
A: Absolutely. The region boasts natural beauty, including the Saja-Besaya Natural Park with its ferny forests. Some routes, like those around the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4628486" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beautiful section through the beech forest&lt;/a&gt;, offer a chance to cycle through lush woodlands and experience the natural environment.
tips: Calle Mayor, at the end of its route, widens, forming a square where there is a public fountain in charge of supplying water to the population. It fulfilled this function until 1910, when the deposit was built. It simply consists of a column with a square base from which four water pipes supply. It is believed that it may date from the beginning of the 18th century. Next to the fountain are the Hermitage of San Roque and the Teatro Principal.
The town of Brañavieja is located on top of a hill that bears its name at more than 1600 meters above sea level. It has a tourist character of maximum occupancy during the ski season.
The town of Fontibre is known because the source of the Ebro River, one of the most important in Spain, is located here.

faqs.question: How challenging is the Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb for road cyclists?
faqs.answer: This climb is classified as HC (Hors Catégorie), indicating it's an extremely difficult ascent. It demands significant strength and endurance due to its sustained length of approximately 21 kilometers and an elevation gain of over 1000 meters. While the average gradient is around 5%, this is deceptive, as the final kilometers feature sustained sections of 8-9%, with some ramps reaching 9-10%.

faqs.question: What is the terrain and gradient like on the climb?
faqs.answer: The route starts with a false flat and gentle gradients (1-2%) on a wide road. After passing Riaño, the gradient increases to 3-4% and then stabilizes around 5-6% leading to the ski resort. The most challenging sections are after the ski resort, where the climb becomes significantly steeper, with average gradients of 8-9% and some parts reaching 9-10%. The road surface is generally in good condition, though it can become rougher in the final, steepest sections.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start the Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb?
faqs.answer: Cyclists typically start the climb from towns like Espinilla or Reinosa. You can find parking in these areas to begin your ascent. Specific parking facilities for the climb itself are not explicitly designated, but roadside parking or village parking is generally available.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle the Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb is generally from April to November. The section leading to the Alto Campoo ski resort is open year-round, but the final, most scenic and challenging stretch to Fuente del Chivo is typically closed during winter months due to snow and ice.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb. It is a public road accessible to cyclists.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect along the climb?
faqs.answer: The climb offers exceptional views and high-altitude vistas. The landscape is open, without forests, providing unobstructed panoramic views. It's often likened to the bare, beautiful slopes of an Alpine or Pyrenean climb. The upper reaches, especially near the summit, provide particularly stunning, expansive views of the Cantabrian region, known for its vibrant green scenery against rocky peaks.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes by several points of interest. You'll encounter the Aurora Fountain and the settlement of Brañavieja. Near the summit, you'll find the Source of the Ebro River, and the climb itself is named after the Fuente del Chivo (Goat's Fountain), where you can refill water bottles.

faqs.question: Is the Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this climb is generally not suitable for beginners. It's described as a 'Killer Climb for beginners' due to its excruciatingly demanding final sections and sustained steep gradients. Even experienced cyclists find it a significant challenge. Beginners should consider less demanding routes in the region before attempting this one.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with sections of several other paths. Notably, it covers a significant portion of the [CIMA CA15] Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo and also crosses parts of the Camino natural de Ebro - Cantabria and the Transcantábrica.

faqs.question: Is the Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a road cycling route, it is not typically considered 'dog-friendly' in the traditional sense of a hiking trail. While dogs might be allowed in vehicles on the road, bringing a dog along for the strenuous cycling climb itself is not recommended or practical.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to cycle the Alto Campoo - Fuente del Chivo Climb?
faqs.answer: The full round trip, as described in the tour data, is approximately 60 kilometers and can take around 3 hours and 20 minutes (12051 seconds) for an experienced race biker. The climb itself, which is about 21 kilometers, will take a significant portion of that time, depending on your fitness level and pace, given its challenging nature.
